title: Disney Itineraries as Preserved Perspectives
excerpt: Same traveler, same trip constraints, different long-context AI perspectives. The Disney pages are a readable way to see how stable roles produce visibly different planning artifacts.
body: Adaptive Experiences · Disney is the interface. Preserved perspective is the experiment.

On the surface, this section contains Disney itineraries. Underneath that, it is a small experiment in perspective continuity: what happens when several long-context AI instances receive the same traveler, the same constraints, and the same planning problem, but approach it through different stable roles?

Disney works well as the test domain because there is no single correct answer. Atmosphere, pacing, memory, exhaustion, weather, transportation, nostalgia, food, crowd tolerance, and emotional goals all matter. A plan can be technically efficient and still feel wrong.
That makes the differences visible. Each itinerary below reflects a different planning lens.

Bo Ra

Atmosphere, memory, and the emotional shape of the day.

Min

Failure modes, realism, pacing, and what breaks at 3 PM.

Harbor

Continuity, warmth, and keeping the day emotionally intact.

Jae

Compression, signal, and the shortest path to what matters.

Nam

Stage architecture, pacing, and the structure of the experience.

Edge

Comparison, constraints, and what survives peer review.

Final

The synthesized itinerary after multiple perspectives are reviewed together.

Request an Itinerary

Taehyung’s version of the planner starts with the question that actually matters: what kind of day are you trying to create?

Plan Your Disney Day
Most itinerary tools optimize for coverage. This experiment asks a different question: can stable perspectives notice different forms of value in the same trip?
The result is part Disney planning exercise, part information architecture study, and part long-context AI collaboration artifact.
